posts.title: KV Cache Quantization: 6x More Context for Free
posts.subtitle: How compressing an LLM's memory unlocks longer context without new hardware
posts.summary: KV cache quantization shrinks the memory an LLM uses to remember prior tokens, letting servers hold far more context per GPU without hurting accuracy. It's becoming a default lever in production inference stacks.

slides.title: The Hidden Memory Tax
slides.body: Every token an LLM generates re-reads every previous token's key and value vectors. That KV cache is often the single biggest consumer of GPU memory — bigger than the model weights.

slides.title: Context Is Expensive
slides.body: A 128K-token conversation can require tens of gigabytes of KV cache per request. More context means fewer concurrent users per GPU, or costly hardware upgrades.
bullets: KV cache grows linearly with context length
Stored in full 16-bit precision by default
Directly limits batch size and concurrency

slides.title: What Quantization Does
slides.body: Instead of storing each key/value number in 16 bits, quantization rounds it to a smaller number of bits (e.g. 3-4) while preserving the range it represents.
bullets: Trades numeric precision for memory savings
Applied per-channel or per-block to limit error
Decoded back to full precision just before attention math

slides.title: Why It (Mostly) Works
slides.body: KV cache values tend to cluster in a narrow range, so aggressive quantization loses very little signal — recent techniques report near-zero measured accuracy loss even at 3 bits.
bullets: Smart calibration keeps outlier values accurate
Attention scores are surprisingly tolerant of rounding

slides.title: Real-World Payoff
slides.body: Google's TurboQuant approach (2026) compresses KV cache to 3 bits with no measured accuracy loss, delivering roughly 6x memory reduction — so one GPU can serve far more long-context requests.
bullets: Stacks with speculative decoding and paged attention

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: KV cache, not weights, is often the real memory bottleneck
Quantizing it trades a sliver of precision for major capacity gains
3-4 bit KV cache quantization is becoming a production default
Pair it with batching and speculative decoding for compounding wins

questions.prompt: What part of an LLM's inference workload does KV cache quantization primarily target?
options: The model's trained weights
The stored key/value vectors from prior tokens
The tokenizer vocabulary
The final output logits
questions.explanation: KV cache holds the key/value vectors for every previous token so attention can reference them; quantization compresses that specific store, not the model weights.

questions.prompt: Why does aggressive KV cache quantization (e.g. 3-4 bits) often lose very little accuracy?
options: LLMs ignore most of the context anyway
KV values cluster in a narrow range, so rounding loses little signal
Quantization only applies to unused tokens
GPUs automatically correct rounding errors
questions.explanation: Key/value activations tend to have a narrow, predictable range, so lower-precision representations retain almost all the useful signal, especially with good calibration.

questions.prompt: What's the main practical benefit of shrinking KV cache memory?
options: Faster model training
Smaller download size for the app
More concurrent long-context requests per GPU
Elimination of hallucinations
questions.explanation: Less memory per request means a server can hold more simultaneous or longer conversations on the same hardware, directly cutting cost per token served.

posts.title: Why 74% of AI Agent Rollouts Get Rolled Back
posts.subtitle: Autonomy without guardrails is the #1 reason enterprise agents fail
posts.summary: As agent spending rockets toward $206B in 2026, most failed deployments trace back to missing governance, not model quality. Guardrails, scoped permissions, and human checkpoints are what separate agents that scale from agents that get pulled.

slides.title: The Agent Rollback Problem
slides.body: AI agent spending is projected to hit $206.5B in 2026 — but a growing share of deployments get built, launched, and then quietly shut off.

slides.title: It's Not the Model
slides.body: Teams assume agent failures are AI-quality problems. Usually they're control problems: nobody defined what the agent is allowed to touch, undo, or escalate.
bullets: No audit trail for autonomous actions
No human checkpoint before high-stakes steps

slides.title: What Governance Means Here
slides.body: Agent governance is the set of rules, permissions, and review points that bound what an autonomous system can do — before it does something costly.
bullets: Logging every decision and tool call

slides.title: Guardrails That Actually Work
slides.body: Enterprises that scale agents budget for external validation and staged autonomy instead of flipping agents to full autonomy on day one.
bullets: Require approval above a cost/impact threshold
Continuously monitor for drift from intended behavior

slides.title: One Process at a Time
slides.body: Winning teams map a single messy workflow, add a human review step, and prove measurable time saved or errors reduced before expanding scope.
bullets: Only widen scope once trust is earned

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Most agent failures are governance failures, not model failures
Least-privilege access and staged autonomy prevent costly surprises
Human checkpoints on irreversible actions build trust fast
Prove value on one narrow workflow before scaling out

questions.prompt: According to the trend, what is the most common reason enterprise AI agent deployments get rolled back?
options: The underlying model is too slow
Lack of governance and guardrails, not model quality
Agents are too expensive to run
Users don't understand AI
questions.explanation: Analyses point to missing governance — undefined permissions, no oversight — as the key differentiator between agents that scale and those that get pulled.

questions.prompt: What does 'least-privilege' mean in the context of AI agent tool access?
options: Giving the agent every available tool for flexibility
Granting only the minimum permissions needed for a specific task
Running the agent without any tools
Letting the agent request new permissions automatically
questions.explanation: Least-privilege scoping limits an agent to exactly the tools and data it needs, shrinking the blast radius if it misbehaves or is misused.

questions.prompt: What's a recommended first step when rolling out a new autonomous agent in production?
options: Give it full autonomy immediately to move fast
Start with read-only or low-impact actions and expand gradually
Skip logging to reduce latency
Deploy it across all workflows at once
questions.explanation: Staged autonomy — starting narrow and expanding only after trust is established — is what separates deployments that scale from ones that get rolled back.

references.title: Top 13 Agentic AI Trends to Watch in 2026

posts.title: Flagship, Mid, Economy: AI Labs Now Ship 3 Model Tiers
posts.subtitle: Picking the right-sized model is now a real cost lever, not an afterthought
posts.summary: Frontier labs increasingly ship a flagship, mid, and economy model side by side. Choosing the cheapest tier that clears your accuracy bar is now core to building AI products profitably.

slides.title: One Model No Longer Fits All
slides.body: This week's frontier releases arrived in three tiers, not one — a flagship for hard agentic work, a mid-tier for everyday business tasks, and a budget tier for high-volume, latency-sensitive jobs.

slides.title: The One-Model Trap
slides.body: Routing every request to the biggest, most expensive model wastes money on tasks that don't need frontier reasoning — and can even be slower.
bullets: Flagship models cost far more per token
Not every task needs top-tier reasoning
Latency-sensitive apps suffer from oversized models

slides.title: The Three-Tier Pattern
slides.body: Labs now typically ship a flagship for complex agentic and coding work, a mid-tier matching last generation's flagship at a fraction of the cost, and a fast, cheap tier for simple, high-volume tasks.
bullets: Flagship: hardest reasoning, coding, agentic tasks
Mid-tier: strong general performance, lower cost
Economy: speed and cost above all

slides.title: This Week's Lineup
slides.body: OpenAI's July releases illustrate the pattern: a flagship built for agentic coding and science tasks, a mid-tier matching prior flagship quality at half the price, and a low-latency budget option.
bullets: Flagship priced for the hardest agentic workloads
Mid-tier: prior-gen flagship quality, lower cost
Economy: optimized for speed and volume

slides.title: How to Choose
slides.body: Benchmark your actual task against the cheapest tier first. Only escalate to a pricier tier when accuracy, not cost, becomes the bottleneck.
bullets: Start cheap, measure quality against your bar
Reserve flagship calls for genuinely hard steps
Mix tiers within one agentic pipeline

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Frontier labs now ship flagship/mid/economy tiers, not one model
Matching task difficulty to tier is a real cost lever
Mid-tier models often match last year's flagship for far less
Multi-tier routing inside one pipeline is increasingly standard

questions.prompt: Why do AI labs increasingly release multiple model tiers instead of one flagship model?
options: To confuse competitors
Different tasks need different tradeoffs between cost, speed, and reasoning depth
Regulations require at least three models
Smaller models are always more accurate
questions.explanation: Not every task needs frontier-level reasoning; tiered releases let developers match cost and latency to what each task actually requires.

questions.prompt: What's a smart strategy for choosing which model tier to use for a given task?
options: Always use the flagship model for consistency
Start with the cheapest tier and escalate only if quality falls short
Always use the cheapest tier regardless of accuracy
Randomly alternate between tiers
questions.explanation: Benchmarking against the cheapest tier first and escalating only when needed keeps costs down without sacrificing quality where it matters.

questions.prompt: In a multi-step agentic pipeline, what does 'mixing tiers' mean?
options: Using a different vendor for each step
Routing easy steps to cheaper models and hard steps to the flagship model
Running every step twice for verification
Only using the economy tier for all steps
questions.explanation: Multi-tier routing sends simple or high-volume steps to cheaper/faster models while reserving the flagship model for the hardest reasoning steps, optimizing cost across the whole pipeline.

posts.title: MCP Goes Stateless: The July RC Rewrite
posts.subtitle: No more sticky sessions — MCP servers finally play nice with load balancers
posts.summary: The July 2026 MCP release candidate strips session state out of the protocol, so any server instance can answer any request. That means remote MCP servers can finally sit behind a plain load balancer before the spec locks on July 28.

slides.title: MCP Goes Stateless
slides.body: A protocol-level rewrite lands this week — and it changes how you'll deploy every remote MCP server you run.

slides.title: The old MCP had a sticky problem
bullets: Sessions lived inside one server process
Load balancers had to pin you to that exact instance
Scale out = session affinity hacks everywhere
One instance dies, your whole session dies with it

slides.title: What 'stateless' actually removes
slides.body: The RC deletes session state from the protocol layer. The server no longer remembers you between calls — the client carries the context instead.

slides.title: Before vs. after, structurally
bullets: Before: client → LB → pinned server → in-memory session
After: client → LB → any healthy server → context in the request
Crashes just mean a retry, not a lost session

rows: Works behind a plain round-robin LB

slides.title: Deploying a remote server this week
slides.body: Drop server replicas behind nginx or an ALB with no session affinity rule. Any node can pick up the next request — no shared session store required.

slides.title: Before July 28
bullets: Audit servers for in-memory session assumptions
Remove sticky-session config from your LB
Push per-call context into the request payload
Re-test failover — a dead node should now be a non-event

questions.prompt: What does the July 2026 MCP RC remove at the protocol level?
options: Tool call authentication
Session state
JSON-RPC message framing
Resource discovery
questions.explanation: The rewrite removes protocol-level session state, so no single server instance owns a client's context anymore.

questions.prompt: Why did stateful MCP servers struggle behind load balancers?
options: They required sticky sessions pinned to one instance
They couldn't use HTTPS
They didn't support JSON
They required a database
questions.explanation: Since session data lived in one process's memory, the load balancer had to route every request from a client back to that same instance.

questions.prompt: In the stateless model, where does per-call context now live?
options: In a shared server-side cache only
It's discarded between calls
Carried in the request itself
In the load balancer's memory
questions.explanation: The client includes the necessary context with each request, so any healthy server instance can process it independently.

posts.title: Context Engineering: Why Agents Fail
posts.subtitle: The model didn't get dumber. Its window got messier.
posts.summary: As agents run longer, the context window fills with stale tool outputs, contradictions, and irrelevant history — and that clutter, not the model's intelligence, is what breaks reasoning. Great agent builders now spend most of their effort curating what enters the window at each step, not wordsmithing the initial prompt.

slides.title: Context Engineering
slides.body: Why agents fail long before the model does.

slides.title: The Model Isn't The Bottleneck Anymore
bullets: Same model, wildly different agent results
The real variable: what's IN the context window
Bad context in → bad output out, every time

slides.title: What Is Context Engineering?
slides.body: Not how you phrase one prompt — the ongoing discipline of deciding what enters the window at every single step of an agent's run.

slides.title: Four Ways Context Breaks
bullets: Poisoning — one bad fact gets referenced forever
Distraction — too much history buries the signal
Confusion — irrelevant tools/docs mislead the model
Clash — conflicting facts fight each other

rows: Optimizes the wording of one message
Optimizes what's loaded at each step
Focus: memory, tools, retrieved docs, history

slides.title: A Real Agent Loop, Turn By Turn
bullets: Step 12: + 40 tool outputs, stack traces, dead plans
Step 20: model 'forgets' the goal — it's buried

slides.title: Engineer The Window, Not Just The Words
bullets: Prune aggressively after every tool call
Compress and summarize instead of appending forever
Isolate sub-tasks into fresh sub-agent contexts

questions.prompt: In mature 2026 agentic frameworks, what has become the primary bottleneck for agent reliability?
options: The model's raw reasoning ability
What gets loaded into the context window at each step
The programming language used to build the agent
GPU inference speed
questions.explanation: Model capability has plateaued relative to agent failures — the deciding factor is now what content the agent stuffs into its own context at every turn.

questions.prompt: What is 'context poisoning'?
options: When a hallucinated or wrong fact from an earlier step keeps getting referenced in later steps
When the model is prompted in an unsupported language
When the context window exceeds the token limit
When two agents share the same API key
questions.explanation: Once an error enters the window, the model treats it as established fact and builds on it, compounding the mistake across subsequent steps.

questions.prompt: What's a practical fix for context distraction?
options: Add more tool descriptions to the system prompt
Compress and prune history, and retrieve just-in-time instead of dumping everything upfront
Increase the model's temperature
Switch to a smaller, cheaper model
questions.explanation: Distraction happens when accumulated history drowns out the actual task — active pruning and lazy retrieval keep the signal-to-noise ratio high.

references.title: Effective context engineering for AI agents

posts.title: Speculative Decoding: LLMs Guess Ahead to Go Faster
posts.subtitle: A small model guesses. A big model checks. Speed without a dumber answer.
posts.summary: Speculative decoding pairs a fast draft model with a big verifier model so LLMs emit several tokens per step instead of one, cutting latency and cost while the target model's output distribution stays mathematically identical.

slides.title: Speculative Decoding
slides.body: The inference trick letting newer, faster model tiers keep up with the smart ones.

slides.title: One Token at a Time
slides.body: LLMs write sequentially. Each token has to wait for the last one to finish before it can even start.
bullets: Latency and cost scale with output length
Every token pays the full forward-pass price

slides.title: Two Models, One Job
bullets: A small, cheap draft model guesses several tokens ahead
A big target model checks all those guesses at once
Checking is parallel — writing never was

slides.title: Guess, Then Verify
slides.body: The draft model proposes a run of tokens. The target model scores the whole run in one forward pass, as if it had written it alone.
bullets: First wrong guess: rejected, target resamples from there

slides.title: Where You've Already Felt It
slides.body: Fast-tier releases like Claude's speed-optimized variants and rumored tiers like GPT-5.6's Luna lean on tricks like this to cut cost-per-token while keeping the flagship model's judgment.

rows: Up to 5-8 tokens verified per pass
Draft model absorbs most of the compute

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Speed comes from guess-then-verify, not from shrinking the model
Rejection sampling guarantees quality never drops
This is why new 'fast' model tiers can feel instant without feeling dumb

questions.prompt: In speculative decoding, what do the draft and target models each do?
options: The draft model guesses several tokens ahead; the target model verifies them all in one parallel pass
Both models split the vocabulary between them and generate different words
The target model trains the draft model in real time during inference
The draft model permanently replaces the target model once it's accurate enough
questions.explanation: The small draft model proposes a run of tokens; the large target model checks the entire run in a single forward pass and keeps only what it agrees with.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't speculative decoding make outputs lower quality?
options: It quietly lowers the sampling temperature
It only ever outputs tokens the draft model produced
The target model's own probabilities decide acceptance via rejection sampling, so the final output distribution matches running the target model alone
It rounds each response to the nearest cached answer
questions.explanation: Rejection sampling mathematically guarantees the accepted tokens come from the same distribution the target model would have produced on its own.

questions.prompt: What happens when the draft model's guess turns out to be wrong?
options: The entire response restarts from the first token
The target model accepts the correct prefix up to the first wrong token, then resamples the rest itself
The draft model is discarded and never used again
The wrong guess is kept anyway to save time
questions.explanation: Only the verified-correct prefix is kept; generation continues from the target model's own resampled token at the point of disagreement.

references.title: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding (Leviathan et al., 2022)
Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling (Chen et al., 2023)

posts.title: Choreography vs Orchestration
posts.subtitle: One conductor waving a baton, or dancers who just know their cues.
posts.summary: Orchestration puts a central brain in charge of every step, trading autonomy for visibility and easy debugging. Choreography lets services react to events independently, trading central control for resilience and loose coupling — the same tradeoff now applies to wiring multi-agent pipelines.

slides.title: Choreography vs Orchestration
slides.body: Two ways to wire services that react to events — and now, the agents that call them.

slides.title: Event-driven systems need a wiring choice
slides.body: Once a system has more than a couple of services reacting to events, someone has to decide how the sequence gets coordinated.
bullets: What happens when a step fails?

slides.title: Orchestration: one conductor
slides.body: A central coordinator calls each service in turn, tracks state, and handles retries.
bullets: Coordinator is a single point of failure

slides.title: Choreography: everyone reacts
slides.body: Services publish events; other services subscribe and act — no one is in charge.
bullets: Resilient — no single point of failure
Hard to see the 'whole story' at a glance

slides.title: For agent pipelines, it's the same call
slides.body: A planner agent dispatching tool-calling sub-agents is orchestration. Agents publishing 'task done' events that trigger the next agent is choreography.
bullets: Debugging a choreographed agent swarm is the hard part

slides.title: Key takeaways
bullets: Orchestration: centralized control, easy visibility, one point of failure
Choreography: decentralized reactions, resilient, hard to trace
Most real systems (and agent pipelines) blend both
Choose based on how much you need to see vs how much you need to scale

questions.prompt: What best describes control flow in an orchestrated system?
options: A central coordinator directs each step in sequence
Services react independently to events with no coordinator
There is no defined flow at all
Services only communicate via shared databases
questions.explanation: Orchestration relies on a central service that explicitly calls each step and tracks the overall state.

questions.prompt: As an agent pipeline grows, what's the main risk of pure choreography?
options: The system becomes a single point of failure
Nobody owns the end-to-end sequence, making it hard to trace what happened
It becomes impossible to add new agents
Events can no longer be published
questions.explanation: Choreography's strength — no central owner — is also its weakness: tracing a full run across many independently reacting agents gets hard.

questions.prompt: Which pattern trades a central dependency for easier debugging and visibility?
options: Choreography
Orchestration
Peer-to-peer messaging
Client-side rendering
questions.explanation: Orchestration centralizes control in one coordinator, which makes the whole workflow easy to inspect and step through — at the cost of that coordinator becoming a dependency.

posts.title: HNSW vs IVF: Choosing a Vector Index That Scales
posts.subtitle: Every RAG demo hides a recall-vs-latency bet it hasn't made yet.
posts.summary: HNSW and IVF are both approximate nearest neighbor indexes, but they trade recall, latency, memory, and build cost differently. Pick HNSW for low-latency real-time search and IVF for massive, cost-constrained datasets — then benchmark on your own embeddings.

slides.title: The Index Nobody Benchmarks Until It's Too Late
slides.body: Behind every vector database is an approximate nearest neighbor index — and the choice between HNSW and IVF decides whether your RAG system survives production.

slides.title: Your Demo Worked. Then You Hit 10M Vectors.
slides.body: Brute-force cosine similarity is exact but scans every vector — fine for 10K rows, fatal for 10M. You need an index that trades a little accuracy for a lot of speed.

slides.title: Every ANN Index Makes the Same Bet
slides.body: Approximate Nearest Neighbor indexes skip most of the dataset on purpose. HOW they decide what to skip shapes recall, latency, memory, and update cost.
bullets: Memory: index size vs raw data

slides.title: HNSW: Navigate a Graph of Shortcuts
slides.body: Hierarchical Navigable Small World builds layered graphs — sparse highways on top, dense local links below — so search hops toward your query in near-log time.

slides.title: IVF: Search Only the Right Neighborhood
slides.body: Inverted File indexing clusters vectors into cells, like k-means, then scans only the few cells closest to your query — skipping the rest entirely.

slides.title: Same Job, Different Tradeoffs
slides.body: Neither index is strictly better — each optimizes for a different failure mode.

slides.title: Pick the Index, Not the Vibe
bullets: HNSW: real-time agent memory, chat RAG, sub-50ms budgets
pgvector, Milvus, Qdrant let you choose per collection
Always benchmark recall@k on your own embeddings

questions.prompt: What does an ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) index trade away to gain speed?
options: Some recall accuracy for lower latency
All accuracy for unlimited scale
Storage space for network bandwidth
Nothing — it's always exact
questions.explanation: ANN indexes intentionally skip parts of the search space, so they may miss some true nearest neighbors in exchange for much faster queries.

questions.prompt: Why does HNSW typically use more memory than IVF?
options: It stores a full graph of neighbor links across multiple layers
It duplicates the entire dataset three times
It requires a separate database for each layer
It stores raw text instead of embeddings
questions.explanation: HNSW's layered graph structure keeps explicit neighbor links per node, which costs more memory than IVF's compact cluster assignments.

questions.prompt: You're building a cost-sensitive index over 2 billion embeddings with relaxed latency needs. Which is the more natural starting point?
options: IVF (often combined with product quantization)
HNSW with maximum ef_construction
Brute-force exact search
A single flat in-memory array
questions.explanation: IVF's compact clustered structure, often paired with quantization, scales more cheaply to billions of vectors than HNSW's memory-heavy graph.

references.title: Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Using HNSW Graphs
Faiss: A Library for Efficient Similarity Search

posts.title: How Vector Databases Power RAG
posts.subtitle: Turning meaning into geometry so LLMs can retrieve facts
posts.summary: Vector databases store embeddings and use approximate nearest-neighbor search to fetch the most relevant chunks for a query — the retrieval half of RAG.

slides.title: Vector Databases
slides.body: The retrieval engine behind Retrieval-Augmented Generation.

slides.title: LLMs forget
slides.body: Models can't see your private docs and have a fixed knowledge cutoff.

slides.title: Embed, then search
slides.body: Convert text to vectors that capture meaning, then find the closest ones to the query.

slides.title: The RAG loop
bullets: Chunks are stuffed into the prompt

questions.prompt: What does a vector database actually store?
options: Raw SQL rows
Embeddings of text chunks
Model weights
Prompt templates
questions.explanation: It stores embeddings plus metadata for nearest-neighbor retrieval.

questions.prompt: Why use ANN instead of exact search?
options: It is more accurate
It scales to millions of vectors fast
It needs no index
It avoids embeddings
questions.explanation: Approximate nearest-neighbor trades a little accuracy for large speed gains.

references.title: Pinecone: What is a vector database?

posts.title: The CAP Theorem, Intuitively
posts.subtitle: Why distributed systems can't have it all
posts.summary: Under a network partition, a distributed system must choose between consistency and availability — you can't guarantee both.

slides.title: CAP Theorem
slides.body: Pick two: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.

slides.title: The three letters
bullets: Consistency: every read sees the latest write
Availability: every request gets a response

slides.title: Partitions aren't optional
slides.body: Networks fail, so P is mandatory. The real choice is C vs A during a partition.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: CAP is about behavior during partitions
CP refuses to serve stale data
AP stays up and reconciles later

questions.prompt: During a partition, CAP forces a choice between…
options: Speed and cost
Consistency and availability
Latency and throughput
SQL and NoSQL
questions.explanation: P is a given on real networks, so you trade C against A.

posts.title: Docker Images vs Containers
posts.subtitle: The blueprint and the running house
posts.summary: An image is an immutable, layered template; a container is a running, writable instance of that image.

slides.title: Layers all the way down
slides.body: Each Dockerfile instruction adds a cached, read-only layer.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Build an image once, run many containers
Containers add a thin writable layer

posts.title: What Model Context Protocol Solves
posts.subtitle: USB-C for connecting AI to your tools and data
posts.summary: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI apps connect to tools, data, and prompts — one interface instead of N bespoke integrations.

slides.title: Model Context Protocol
slides.body: A universal connector between LLM apps and the outside world.

slides.title: The N×M problem
slides.body: Every app re-implements every integration. It doesn't scale.

slides.title: One protocol
slides.body: Servers expose tools, resources, and prompts. Clients speak one language to all of them.

slides.title: Client ↔ Server
bullets: Host app embeds an MCP client

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Build a server once, reuse everywhere

questions.prompt: Which is NOT a core MCP primitive?
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Gradients
questions.explanation: MCP exposes tools, resources, and prompts.

posts.title: Database Indexes Without the Mystery
posts.subtitle: Why a B-tree turns a scan into a lookup
posts.summary: An index is a sorted structure that lets the database jump straight to matching rows instead of scanning the whole table.

slides.title: Database Indexes
slides.body: The difference between O(n) and O(log n) at the table level.

slides.title: Full table scans
slides.body: Without an index, WHERE checks every row on disk.

slides.title: The B-tree
slides.body: A balanced, sorted tree lets lookups descend in logarithmic steps.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Every index has a write cost

questions.prompt: What is the main downside of adding an index?
options: Reads get slower
Writes get slower and use more storage
Queries stop working
Data is lost
questions.explanation: Indexes must be maintained on every write and consume storage.

posts.title: Kubernetes in One Mental Model
posts.subtitle: Declare desired state; the control loop reconciles
posts.summary: You declare the desired state of your workloads; Kubernetes controllers continuously reconcile reality toward that state.

slides.title: Kubernetes
slides.body: A control loop for containers.

slides.title: Desired vs actual
slides.body: You write YAML describing what should be true. Controllers make it true.

posts.title: Prompt Engineering That Scales
posts.subtitle: Structure beats clever wording
posts.summary: Reliable prompts are structured: clear role, explicit task, constraints, and examples — not magic phrasing.

slides.title: Prompt Engineering
slides.body: Design the input like an API contract.

slides.title: Few-shot wins
slides.body: Two or three good examples usually beat a paragraph of instructions.

questions.prompt: What most reliably improves output quality?
options: Longer prose instructions
A few concrete examples
All caps emphasis
Politeness
questions.explanation: Few-shot examples anchor the model's behavior more than wording tricks.

posts.title: Idempotency for Reliable APIs
posts.subtitle: Make retries safe by design
posts.summary: An idempotent endpoint produces the same result no matter how many times an identical request is retried — essential for safe distributed systems.

slides.title: Idempotency
slides.body: Retry without fear of double charges.

slides.title: Networks lie
slides.body: A client can't tell a lost response from a failed request, so it retries.

slides.title: Idempotency keys
slides.body: The client sends a unique key; the server stores the result and replays it on retry.

questions.prompt: How do you make a POST safe to retry?
options: Add a unique idempotency key
Increase the timeout
Use HTTPS
Cache the DNS
questions.explanation: An idempotency key lets the server dedupe repeated identical requests.
